summary:
The Epic of Gilgamesh is the oldest known work of epic literature in the world, with its origins traceable to the Early Dynastic Period of Mesopotamia around 2700–2500 BC. Gilgamesh himself appears to have been a historical king of the Sumerian city of Uruk, and within a generation or two of his reign he had become a legendary figure. The earliest Sumerian poems about Gilgamesh circulated independently before being synthesised into a unified epic narrative, a process that unfolded over several centuries and culminated in the Standard Babylonian Version inscribed on twelve tablets around 1200 BC.

The epic narrates the adventures of Gilgamesh, king of Uruk, who is described as two-thirds divine and one-third human. In partnership with the wild man Enkidu, created by the gods to be his companion and equal, Gilgamesh performs heroic deeds including slaying the monster Humbaba in the Cedar Forest and killing the Bull of Heaven. When Enkidu dies as punishment for these transgressions, Gilgamesh is devastated and embarks on a quest for immortality, driven by a terror of death that was entirely new in literary history.

Gilgamesh's quest for immortality leads him to the edges of the world, where he finds the sole survivor of a great flood — the immortal Utnapishtim — whose story contains a detailed flood narrative that scholars have long compared to the biblical story of Noah. The parallels are striking: a divine warning of the flood, the construction of a great boat, the loading of animals, the sending out of birds to find land, and the granting of divine favour. This parallel demonstrates the direct literary influence of Mesopotamian traditions on later biblical and Near Eastern literature.

The epic's exploration of themes — friendship, mortality, the search for meaning, the limits of human power, the consolations of civilised life over the pursuit of immortality — gives it a psychological and philosophical depth that has struck readers across millennia as remarkably modern. The wise tavern-keeper Siduri's advice to Gilgamesh (enjoy life, tend to family and work, for immortality is for the gods alone) anticipates the philosophical traditions of later Greek, Roman, and Near Eastern thought.

The rediscovery of the Epic of Gilgamesh in the nineteenth century, when George Smith of the British Museum recognised the flood narrative on tablet XI in 1872, caused a sensation in the scholarly and theological world. The text has since been translated into virtually every major language and has profoundly influenced modern understanding of the relationship between ancient Mesopotamian and biblical literary traditions. It stands as one of humanity's foundational stories and a testament to the literary genius of the Sumerian civilisation that created it.
